exercises.title: Classify space complexity
exercises.instructions:
Classify space complexity for each:
A: `int x = 5;` inside a loop over n elements
B: `int[] copy = arr.clone();` (arr has n elements)
C: `Map<String,Integer> map = new HashMap<>();` (n entries)
D: Recursive function with depth log n
E: Recursive function with depth n
F: `int[][] matrix = new int[n][n];`
exercises.expected_solution:
A: O(1) — single variable regardless of loop iterations
B: O(n) — creates copy of n-element array
C: O(n) — n key-value pairs stored in HashMap
D: O(log n) — log n stack frames on call stack
E: O(n) — n stack frames on call stack (e.g. linear recursion)
F: O(n²) — n×n matrix = n² cells

test_cases.expected:
A: O(1) — single variable regardless of loop iterations
B: O(n) — creates copy of n-element array
C: O(n) — n key-value pairs stored in HashMap
D: O(log n) — log n stack frames on call stack
E: O(n) — n stack frames on call stack (e.g. linear recursion)
F: O(n²) — n×n matrix = n² cells

hints: Scalar variable: O(1) always
Clone of n-element array: O(n)
Recursion depth = stack space

exercises.title: Time AND space for common algorithms
exercises.instructions:
State both time AND space complexity:
A: Iterative binary search
B: Recursive binary search
C: Merge sort
D: Heapsort
E: HashMap-based frequency count (n elements)
exercises.expected_solution:
A: Iterative binary search:
   Time: O(log n) — halving search space
   Space: O(1) — loop variables only, no recursion

B: Recursive binary search:
   Time: O(log n) — same halving
   Space: O(log n) — log n stack frames at max depth

C: Merge sort:
   Time: O(n log n) — divide + linear merge
   Space: O(n) — temporary arrays for merging (+ O(log n) stack)

D: Heapsort:
   Time: O(n log n) — heap build + n extractions
   Space: O(1) — in-place, no extra arrays, iterative

E: HashMap frequency count:
   Time: O(n) — one pass
   Space: O(n) — stores up to n unique keys

test_cases.expected:
A: Iterative binary search:
   Time: O(log n) — halving search space
   Space: O(1) — loop variables only, no recursion

B: Recursive binary search:
   Time: O(log n) — same halving
   Space: O(log n) — log n stack frames at max depth

C: Merge sort:
   Time: O(n log n) — divide + linear merge
   Space: O(n) — temporary arrays for merging (+ O(log n) stack)

D: Heapsort:
   Time: O(n log n) — heap build + n extractions
   Space: O(1) — in-place, no extra arrays, iterative

E: HashMap frequency count:
   Time: O(n) — one pass
   Space: O(n) — stores up to n unique keys

hints: Iterative = O(1) space. Recursive = O(depth) space
Merge sort temp arrays: O(n) space
Heapsort: true O(1) — in-place rearrangement

exercises.title: Measure actual memory: in-place vs extra space
exercises.instructions:
Implement two array reversal methods:
1. In-place: O(1) extra space — swap elements
2. Extra space: O(n) — create new array

Verify both give same result. Print 'In-place uses O(1) space' and 'Extra copy uses O(n) space'.
hints: In-place: lo/hi pointers, single temp variable = O(1)
Copy: new int[arr.length] = O(n) space
Two-pointer reversal: classic O(1) space pattern

exercises.title: Recursion stack depth = space complexity
exercises.instructions:
Show that recursive function depth = stack space.
1. countDown(n): prints n, n-1, ..., 0 recursively
2. Track max stack depth using a counter
3. Show that for n=100: max depth = 101 (n+1) → O(n) space
4. Convert to iterative O(1) space version
hints: Track currentDepth: increment on enter, decrement on return
maxDepth at any point = current stack depth
Iterative: same algorithm, loop instead of recursion → O(1) space

exercises.title: Memoization: time-space trade-off
exercises.instructions:
Demonstrate the time-space trade-off for Fibonacci:
1. O(2ⁿ) time, O(n) space (naive recursion)
2. O(n) time, O(n) space (memoization — adds array)
3. O(n) time, O(1) space (iterative — best of both)

Compute fib(40) with all three. Show trade-offs.
hints: Memoization: store in long[] array, O(n) space
Iterative: only prev + curr needed, O(1) space
n=35 naive: ~29M calls — measurably slow

exercises.title: Space complexity of HashMap-based solutions
exercises.instructions:
Show when HashMap adds O(n) space vs when it doesn't change complexity:

Problem: given array, find if any element appears more than once.
Solution A: nested loops (O(n²) time, O(1) space)
Solution B: HashSet (O(n) time, O(n) space)

Implement both. Print complexity. Discuss the trade-off.
hints: seen.add() returns false if already present — elegant duplicate check
HashSet: uses O(n) space for up to n elements
Trade-off: always consider memory constraints

exercises.title: In-place array operations
exercises.instructions:
Implement these in-place (O(1) extra space) — no creating new arrays:
1. removeZeros(int[] arr): move all zeros to the end, maintain relative order of non-zeros
   Example: [0,1,0,2,3] → [1,2,3,0,0]
2. Print time AND space complexity for your solution
hints: writePos: tracks where next non-zero goes
Two passes: move non-zeros forward, then fill zeros backward
This pattern (write pointer) is the standard in-place filter

exercises.title: Space-time trade-off: caching expensive computations
exercises.instructions:
Zerodha needs to compute P&L for each merchant repeatedly.
Computing P&L is expensive: O(n) per merchant.

1. Naive: compute on demand → O(n) time per query
2. Cache (memoize): compute once, cache → O(1) per subsequent query
   But uses O(m) extra space where m = unique merchants

Implement both. Show time savings at cost of space.
hints: computeIfAbsent: compute only on cache miss, O(1) on hit
O(m) space for m merchants — usually tiny compared to n
Caching: classic time-space trade-off in production systems

exercises.title: Space of string operations
exercises.instructions:
Analyze space complexity of string operations:
1. reverse(String s): naive (new array) vs in-place (char[])
2. palindrome check: O(1) space two-pointer vs O(n) space reversed string comparison
3. Print space complexity and verify same results
hints: String immutability: reversal always needs at least O(n) for char[]
Palindrome O(1): two pointers, no copy needed
charAt(i): O(1) — no extra space

exercises.title: Space for graph algorithms
exercises.instructions:
State time AND space complexity for:
A: BFS on graph with V vertices, E edges (uses Queue)
B: DFS on graph with V vertices (uses recursion)
C: Adjacency matrix storage for V vertices
D: Adjacency list storage for V vertices and E edges
E: Dijkstra's algorithm (priority queue based)
exercises.expected_solution:
A: BFS — O(V+E) time, O(V) space
   Queue stores at most V vertices at any time
   Visited set: O(V)
   Total extra space: O(V)
   Time: visits each vertex once (V) + each edge once (E)

B: DFS recursive — O(V+E) time, O(V) space
   Recursion depth: O(V) in worst case (linear graph)
   Visited array: O(V)
   Total extra space: O(V) call stack + O(V) visited = O(V)

C: Adjacency matrix — O(V²) space
   V×V boolean/int matrix
   Suitable for dense graphs (E ≈ V²)
   Fast edge check O(1), but wastes space for sparse graphs

D: Adjacency list — O(V+E) space
   V lists + total E edges across all lists
   Better for sparse graphs (E << V²)
   Java: ArrayList[] or Map<Integer,List<Integer>>

E: Dijkstra's (priority queue) — O(E log V) time, O(V+E) space
   Priority queue: O(V) entries at any time
   Distance array: O(V)
   With adjacency list: O(V+E) total space

test_cases.expected:
A: BFS — O(V+E) time, O(V) space
   Queue stores at most V vertices at any time
   Visited set: O(V)
   Total extra space: O(V)
   Time: visits each vertex once (V) + each edge once (E)

B: DFS recursive — O(V+E) time, O(V) space
   Recursion depth: O(V) in worst case (linear graph)
   Visited array: O(V)
   Total extra space: O(V) call stack + O(V) visited = O(V)

C: Adjacency matrix — O(V²) space
   V×V boolean/int matrix
   Suitable for dense graphs (E ≈ V²)
   Fast edge check O(1), but wastes space for sparse graphs

D: Adjacency list — O(V+E) space
   V lists + total E edges across all lists
   Better for sparse graphs (E << V²)
   Java: ArrayList[] or Map<Integer,List<Integer>>

E: Dijkstra's (priority queue) — O(E log V) time, O(V+E) space
   Priority queue: O(V) entries at any time
   Distance array: O(V)
   With adjacency list: O(V+E) total space

hints: BFS queue max size: O(V)
Adjacency matrix: V² space even for sparse graphs
Adjacency list: O(V+E) — only stores actual edges

exercises.title: Reverse a linked list — O(1) space in-place
exercises.instructions:
Reverse a singly linked list in-place (O(1) extra space).
Naive approach: copy to ArrayList, reverse, rebuild (O(n) space).
Optimal: three-pointer trick (O(1) space).

Implement both. Verify same result. Print space complexity.
hints: Three pointers: prev=null, cur=head, next=cur.next
cur.next = prev: reverse the link direction
prev becomes the new head after the loop

exercises.title: Space complexity of dynamic programming
exercises.instructions:
Longest Common Subsequence (LCS) — classic DP:
1. 2D DP table: O(m×n) space
2. Optimized: only 2 rows needed → O(n) space
3. Further: only 1 row with careful update → O(n) space

Implement 2D and optimized. Verify same LCS length. Print space improvement.
hints: LCS only needs previous row: swap prev/curr each iteration
O(n) space: only 2 arrays of length n+1 instead of m×n matrix
Common DP optimization: identify which rows/cols are actually needed

exercises.title: Find Kth Largest Element — O(1) space
exercises.instructions:
Find Kth largest element in unsorted array.
1. Sort approach: O(n log n) time, O(1) or O(n) space (depends on sort)
2. Min-heap of size K: O(n log k) time, O(k) space
3. QuickSelect: O(n) average time, O(1) space

Implement all three. Verify same result for k=3 in [3,2,1,5,6,4].
hints: Kth largest = (n-k)th smallest in sorted order (0-indexed)
Min-heap of size k: always contains k largest seen so far, peek = kth largest
QuickSelect: partition like quicksort, recurse only on relevant half

exercises.title: Space-optimal palindrome partition
exercises.instructions:
Check if a string can be made palindrome by removing at most one character.
Example: 'raceacar' → remove one 'a' → 'racecar' (palindrome) → true

1. Naive O(n) space: create substring, check
2. Optimal O(1) space: two pointers with recursive check on mismatch

Implement both. Test with multiple strings. Print space for each.
hints: checkPalin(s, lo, hi): check range without creating substring
Passing indices instead of substrings: O(1) extra space vs O(n)
This pattern (pass range instead of create substring) saves space repeatedly

exercises.title: Complete M29 T3 Space Complexity Synthesis
exercises.instructions:
Build complete space complexity synthesis:
1. Implement 4 algorithms with DIFFERENT space complexities:
   - O(1): count even numbers in array
   - O(log n): recursive binary search
   - O(n): frequency HashMap
   - O(n²): 2D DP table
2. For each: print 'Time: O(?) Space: O(?)'
3. @SpringBootTest equivalent (4 tests): all correct, space correctly classified
hints: O(1) space: only scalar variables
O(log n) space: recursion depth for binary search
O(n) space: HashMap or array of size n
O(m×n) space: 2D DP table
